title: Don't Repeat Yourself - Loops and Efficiency
description: Students learn what loops are, why they make code efficient, and how to debug looping bugs.
learningObjectives: Explain what a loop is and why programmers use loops instead of repeating the same line many times.
Identify repetition in everyday routines and technology examples.
Use and explain key vocabulary: loop, iteration, bug, debug, efficient.
Spot a bug in a loop and suggest a fix.
prerequisites: Lesson 1 - students already understand algorithms and sequences.
materials: Paper and pencils for every student
Optional sticky notes or index cards for loop cards activity
Clear space at the front for physical activity
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain B (22B). Builds on Lesson 1 by extending sequence thinking into repetition and efficiency. Role model: Grace Hopper, who helped build early programming languages and documented the first actual computer bug, giving us the terms bug and debug.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson: Loop, Iteration, Bug, Debug, Efficient. Prepare open floor space for the human loop and Fortnite dance debugging activity.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question using the brushing-teeth scale example. Capture highest and lowest guesses and reveal why loops are needed.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Human loop warm-up. Run Clap/Stamp/Beep sequence, then rewrite as LOOP 5 TIMES and discuss iteration counting and LOOP FOREVER.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro and loop types. Connect to Lesson 1 sandwich sequence, show efficiency rewrite with LOOP 10 TIMES, and introduce count/until/forever loops at age-appropriate depth.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Fortnite dance bug challenge. Run working loop, then buggy LOOP FOREVER version, then pair debugging with rule constraints and share fixes.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on loop correctness, forever loops in real life, and precision versus efficiency.
Exercises (47-57 min): Complete Exercise 1 (Rewrite with a loop) and Exercise 2 (Spot the bug). Use extension for fast finishers.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Students share corrected loops and complete sentence: 'A loop is useful when _____ and dangerous when _____.'
discussionPrompts: Round 1 worked and Round 2 broke. What changed between the two programs?
Where in real life does something loop forever, and what stops it?
Can code be both precise and efficient at the same time? Why or why not?
If loops make code shorter, why not use loops for everything?
tips: Do not over-focus on exact tooth-brushing math; absurd scale is the learning hook.
The physical loop is key for this age group. Keep it short but concrete.
For high-energy classes, let students mime actions seated instead of standing.
During pair debug work, ask guiding questions about end position rather than giving answers.
Leave the sugar loop question open: how does a machine know when to stop?

teacherGuide: Core message: loops reduce repetition while preserving precision. Students should experience both a working loop and a broken loop physically, then debug with peers. Observe whether students identify the exact source of failure (forever repeat and sequence state mismatch), and whether they can justify fixes clearly.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Rewrite It with a Loop
studentExercises.description: Rewrite the repeated hair-brushing algorithm using loops, then create a more complex loop for left/right/back sections. Keep output the same but code more efficient.
instructions: Start from this long algorithm: Move brush from top of head to bottom (repeated 10 times).
Rewrite it as one count loop that produces the same output.
Then write a second version: brush left side, brush right side, brush back; repeat those three sections three times.
Swap with a partner and check whether both loop versions produce the same result as intended.
If the output changes, debug and rewrite.
successCriteria: Student identifies the repeated unit correctly.
Loop version is shorter but equivalent in output.
Partner can explain why the rewritten loop works.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Spot the Bug
studentExercises.description: Analyze three buggy loops and write fixes: classroom movement logic, infinite sugar loop, and fridge resource leak.
instructions: Buggy loop 1: LOOP 5 TIMES: Stand up; Sit down; Lie on the floor. Decide whether this is a logic bug, sequence bug, or both, and justify your fix.
Buggy loop 2: LOOP FOREVER: Add one sugar to the coffee; Stir. Add a stop condition and discuss how a machine can detect it.
Buggy loop 3: LOOP 3 TIMES: Open fridge; Take out juice; Pour glass. Fix the missing close-fridge step and explain the resource leak problem.
Share one fix with another pair and test whether it removes the bug.
successCriteria: Students identify the core bug in all three loops.
At least one fix includes a clear stop condition.
Students can explain why the corrected version is safer or more correct.

studentExercises.title: Extension - Write a Loop for Something in Your Life
studentExercises.description: Choose a repeated real-life activity and model it as a loop with type, stop condition, and one possible bug plus fix.
instructions: Choose one repeated activity from your life.
Name the loop type: count, until, or forever.
Write the steps inside the loop.
Describe what stops the loop, or explain why it never stops.
Find one possible bug and write a fix.
Challenge: write a loop with another loop inside it (nested loop).
successCriteria: Student can identify and repair one plausible bug.

vocabulary.def: An instruction that repeats a set of steps a set number of times
One single run through the loop
An error in the code that causes the wrong output
Doing the most with the least effort

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these five words on the board and leave them visible all lesson:
Loop — an instruction that repeats a set of steps a certain number of times
Iteration — one single run through the loop
Bug — an error in the code that causes the wrong output
Debug — finding and fixing a bug
Efficient — doing the most with the least effort
Clear a space at the front of the room large enough for students to stand and move — you will need this for Phase 2 and Phase 4.
Have paper and pencils ready for the exercise phase.
lessonSlides.roleModel: Grace Hopper (1906–1992) was a computer scientist and US Navy admiral who helped invent one of the first programming languages. She is also the person who found the very first real computer bug — a moth stuck inside a computer causing errors. She taped it into her notebook and wrote 'First actual case of bug being found.' The words bug and debug come directly from her. Today students will debug their first loop, just like she did. Mention this when you introduce the lesson or at any natural moment.
lessonSlides.tip: This lesson builds directly on Lesson 1. Students already know what an algorithm is and why sequence matters. You do not need to re-teach that. Just briefly connect the two when you reach Phase 3.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "If I asked you to write down every single step of brushing your teeth for an entire year — every stroke, every rinse, every spit — how many lines would you need to write?"
whatToDo: Ask students to turn to the person next to them and make a guess.
Take 3–4 answers from the class.
Write the biggest and smallest guesses on the board.

Then reveal: 'A dentist recommends 2 minutes of brushing twice a day. That is roughly 2 strokes per second — 240 strokes per session, 480 per day, 175,200 per year. Imagine writing 175,200 lines of code just for brushing teeth. There has to be a better way. Today we are going to find it.'
lessonSlides.tip: Do not calculate this precisely in class — the point is the scale, not the exact number. Students finding it absurd is exactly the right reaction. That absurdity is the motivation for loops. If students say 'that is impossible to write out' — tell them they are exactly right, and that is why loops were invented.

lessonSlides.title: The Human Loop
whatToDo: Ask the whole class to stand up.
Write these three steps on the board: Clap once / Stamp your foot / Say 'beep'
Tell the class to run through the three steps once together. They do it.
Then say: 'Now do it again. And again. And again.' Let them run it a few times, then suddenly shout STOP.
Ask: 'How many times did you do those three steps?' They probably lost count.
Now erase the board and rewrite it as: LOOP 5 TIMES: / Clap once / Stamp your foot / Say beep
Run it again. Students count the iterations themselves and stop at 5.
After they stop, ask: 'What was different that time? Why did you know when to stop?'
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "That instruction — do this set of steps exactly X number of times — is a loop. The number five is how many iterations you run. One full run through the three steps is one iteration."
lessonSlides.tip: Do not skip this activity. Students are literally executing a loop with their bodies. The moment they count iterations and stop at exactly 5 is the moment the concept locks in. It takes only 3 minutes and makes the rest of the lesson much easier.
lessonSlides.shyStudentsNote: If the class is very energetic, have students mime the actions at their seats rather than moving around. The concept lands just as well seated.

lessonSlides.title: What Is a Forever Loop?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "What would happen if I wrote LOOP FOREVER instead of LOOP 5 TIMES?"
whatToDo: Let students answer — they will immediately say they would never stop.
Tell them: 'That is exactly what happens when a program has a bug in its loop. It runs forever and crashes the computer. That is called an infinite loop.'
Write LOOP FOREVER on the board next to LOOP 5 TIMES so both are visible.
Ask: 'Can you think of anything in real life that loops forever? When does it stop?' (Heartbeat, clock ticking, a game running in the background.)
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Every time your phone plays music, it does not have a separate instruction for each beat. Every time a game character runs, it does not have a separate instruction for each footstep. Repetition is everywhere in technology, and loops are how programmers handle it without writing the same line hundreds of times.

lessonSlides.title: Loop Types — Count, Until, Forever
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "Remember last week's sandwich algorithm? Some of you wrote something like 'spread the peanut butter across the bread, left to right, covering the whole surface.' How many spreading strokes would that actually be — maybe 8 or 10? A programmer would not write Stroke 1, Stroke 2, Stroke 3 all the way to Stroke 10. They would write: LOOP 10 TIMES: Move knife one stroke across the bread. Same result. Ten times fewer lines of code. That is what loops do — they make code efficient."
whatToDo: Write LOOP 10 TIMES: / Move knife one stroke across the bread on the board.
Now write and explain each loop type — one at a time:
COUNT LOOP — repeat exactly X times. Example: do 10 jumping jacks. You know exactly when it ends.
UNTIL LOOP — repeat until something changes. Example: keep stirring until the sugar dissolves. You do not know how many times — you stop when the condition is met.
FOREVER LOOP — repeat indefinitely. Example: a clock ticking, a game running in the background waiting for you to press a button.

Give one real-world example for each: Count loop: a washing machine spin cycle — spins exactly 800 times. Until loop: a self-driving car braking — keeps applying brakes until speed reaches zero. Forever loop: your phone screen checking every second whether you have a new notification.
lessonSlides.tip: You do not need to go deep on until loops today. The count loop and the forever loop are enough for this age group. The until loop is introduced here so students have the vocabulary, but the challenge focuses on count loops and debugging a forever loop. Keep it brief and move on.
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Ask students: 'When does a video game loop? When does it stop?' They will usually say the game loops forever while you play, but stops when you turn it off or lose all your lives. That is exactly right — games are forever loops with a stop condition buried inside them.

lessonSlides.title: The Fortnite Dance — Working Loop
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "Imagine a game programmer has written a dance emote for a Fortnite character. Here is their code."
whatToDo: Write this on the board: LOOP 3 TIMES: / Dab / Spin / Jump
Have the whole class stand up.
Count the iterations out loud together as students run the code.
After three complete loops, everyone sits down.
Ask: 'Did it work? Did you do exactly what the code said?' (Yes.) 'That is a working loop.'
lessonSlides.tip: This activity comes directly from the Dream Space Unplugged Explorers curriculum and has been tested with this age group. It is physical and funny. Let students enjoy it — the contrast between this working loop and the broken one in Round 2 is what makes the bug concept land.

lessonSlides.title: The Fortnite Dance — Buggy Loop
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "The programmer updated the dance but accidentally introduced a bug. Here is the new code."
whatToDo: Erase the board and write: LOOP FOREVER: / Dab / Spin / Touch your toes
Have students stand and begin running the code.
After the first iteration, they will be bent over touching their toes.
Now try to spin from that position — they cannot. They are stuck.
Let the chaos run for about 30 seconds, then call stop.
Ask: 'What went wrong?' Let students identify the problems themselves — do not give the answer yet.
lessonSlides.tip: Students will identify two problems: the loop runs forever with no stop condition, and step 3 leaves them in a position where step 2 in the next iteration is physically impossible. Wait for them to find both. If they only find one, ask: 'After you touch your toes, can you spin? What position are you starting the next iteration from?'

lessonSlides.title: Debug It in Pairs
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "Your job is to fix the code. You must keep all three dance moves, fix the loop so it ends, and fix the sequence so each step flows into the next."
whatToDo: Give pairs 5 minutes to write their corrected version on paper.
Walk around and ask: 'After step 3, what position are you in? Can you do step 2 from that position?' That question is usually enough to unlock it without giving the answer.
Take 3–4 pairs to share their solution and explain why their fix works.
Accept any solution that works. The simplest correct answer is: LOOP 3 TIMES: Dab, Spin, Jump — because jump returns you to standing for the next iteration.
Some students may add a step 4: Stand up straight, before the loop repeats. That is a perfectly valid debug.
lessonSlides.tip: Do not give the answer before students have tried. The 5 minutes of pair work is where the learning happens. Celebrate creative fixes — there is more than one correct solution. You can name the concept: 'What you are doing right now is called debugging. Professional programmers spend a lot of their time doing exactly this.'

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question below that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than three rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
discussionQuestions: "In Round 1 the loop worked perfectly. In Round 2 it broke. What was the difference between those two programs?" Follow-up: "It was not just the forever loop that broke it — the sequence inside the loop was also wrong. What does that tell us about how loops and sequences work together?"

"Where in real life does something loop forever — and what stops it?" Follow-up: "A heartbeat loops forever — what stops it? A washing machine loops — what stops it? A YouTube video autoplays forever — what stops it? Are all forever loops bad, or are some of them useful?"

"Last week we said computers are precise. Today we said they are also efficient. Can something be both precise and efficient at the same time?" Note: this is the hardest question. For Groep 5–6, use instead: "If loops make code shorter, why do not programmers just use loops for everything?"
lessonSlides.tip: Question 2 — forever loops in real life — is the most accessible and usually generates the best discussion across all ages. The connection between forever loops and everyday cycles (heartbeats, seasons, school timetables) surprises students and makes the concept feel much bigger than coding.

lessonSlides.title: Rewrite It with a Loop
whatToDo: Write this algorithm on the board — tell students it is a robot brushing someone's hair:
Move brush from top of head to bottom (written out 10 times, one per line)
Students work individually to rewrite it as one count loop that produces the same result.
Then ask them to write a second, more complex version: brush left side, brush right side, brush back — and loop through all three sections three times each.
When students finish, they swap papers with a partner.
The partner checks whether the loop version produces the same output as the original long version. If not, they suggest a fix.
lessonSlides.tip: Watch for students who correctly identify the repeated unit (the single brushstroke) and wrap it in a count loop. Students who attempt the complex version and correctly nest the section changes inside the count are showing strong understanding — that is the beginning of nested loops, which appears in later weeks. You do not need to name it now.

lessonSlides.title: Spot the Bug
whatToDo: Present these three buggy loops on the board one at a time. Students work in pairs to find the bug and write a fix.
Buggy loop 1: LOOP 5 TIMES: Stand up / Sit down / Lie on the floor — Ask: is this a logic bug or a sequence bug? Students should argue their case.

Buggy loop 2: LOOP FOREVER: Add one sugar to the coffee / Stir — This loop never stops. Students must add a stop condition. Most will suggest LOOP UNTIL coffee tastes sweet enough. Then ask: how does a machine know when coffee tastes sweet enough? That is the hard question — do not resolve it.

Buggy loop 3: LOOP 3 TIMES: Open fridge / Take out juice / Pour glass — There is no close fridge step. After 3 iterations the fridge has been opened 3 times and never closed. Ask: what is the problem here beyond just being inefficient?
lessonSlides.tip: Buggy loop 2 is the hardest and the most interesting. The question of how a machine knows when something is good enough is genuinely difficult and connects directly to AI and sensor-based computing in later weeks. Leave the question open and write it on the board — do not give an answer. The fridge bug introduces the idea of a resource leak without needing to name it.

lessonSlides.title: Write a Loop for Your Life
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "Choose any repeated activity from your own life and write it as a loop."
rules: Name the activity and the type of loop: count, until, or forever.
Write out the steps inside the loop.
Describe what stops the loop — or explain why it never stops.
Find one possible bug and write the fix.
challengeQuestions: Can you write a loop that has another loop inside it? Think about a weekly school timetable — each week repeats, and inside each week, each day repeats.
If you are stuck on a topic, try one of these: a dog wagging its tail when it hears a noise, a traffic light changing colours, a vending machine dispensing a snack, a teacher marking homework.
lessonSlides.tip: The nested loop challenge (a loop inside a loop) is introduced here informally. Do not over-explain. Students who get there on their own are showing exactly the kind of extended thinking this program is designed to build.

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask 2–3 pairs to read their corrected loop from Exercise 1 or their bug fix from Exercise 2.
After each one, ask the class: 'Would that work? Is it efficient? Is there still a bug hiding in there?'
Keep it short and warm — this is a light share, not a formal presentation.
Ask every student — verbally or on a sticky note — to complete this sentence:
lessonSlides.exitReflection: "A loop is useful when _______ and dangerous when _______."
lessonSlides.whatToSay: After collecting responses or hearing 3–4 answers, close with: "Next week we look at how computers make decisions — not just following a sequence and not just repeating, but actually choosing what to do next based on what is happening. And the tool they use for that is something you already understand: if this, then that."
lessonSlides.watchFor: Did students identify the bug in Round 2 without being told what to look for? Did they connect the physical impossibility — stuck bent over — to the concept of a loop error? In Exercise 2, did students engage with the sugar loop question: how does a machine know when to stop? Students who wrestle genuinely with that question are showing exactly the kind of critical thinking this program is designed to build. Note which students push back on the idea that machines can sense quality — they are the ones to stretch in Lesson 3.

studentChallenges.title: Fortnite Dance Bug Debug
studentChallenges.description: Debug the dance loop so it keeps all three moves, ends correctly, and stays physically executable every iteration.
instructions: Run working code: LOOP 3 TIMES: Dab, Spin, Jump.
Run buggy code: LOOP FOREVER: Dab, Spin, Touch your toes.
In pairs, rewrite code so it has a stop and remains physically possible each iteration.
Keep all three dance moves in your corrected version.
Share and defend your debug choices.
successCriteria: Corrected loop includes a finite stop.
Sequence can be executed repeatedly without getting stuck.
Pair explains why fix works better than original buggy version.

studentChallenges.title: Infinite Loop Rescue
studentChallenges.description: Convert a dangerous forever loop into a safe until or count loop with a measurable stop condition.
instructions: Choose one forever loop example from class.
Rewrite it as either a count loop or an until loop.
State exactly how the program can detect the stop condition.
Test your stop condition by giving a counterexample.
Revise if your loop still risks running forever.
successCriteria: Stop condition is explicit and measurable.
Group can explain how the machine checks stop status.
Rewritten loop avoids infinite repetition in the test case.
